structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
The excerpt is the complete table-of-contents and front matter for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Specification of Raw Data Stream” (Doc-ID 1098).  
It states the document’s purpose (standardised raw-data streaming API for automotive Ethernet), summarizes release changes (new IEEE 1722 AVTP support, renamed error domain, editorial fixes), and lists every normative section: glossary of AVTP/IEEE 1722 terms, requirements trace, functional spec for IP-based and IEEE 1722 streaming, security/access-control model, lifecycle, reporting, detailed C++ API classes (RawDataStreamClient/Server, IEEE1722 datagram types), error-domain, service interfaces, configuration, and change history.

structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary:
The excerpt introduces the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Raw Data Stream” functional cluster, whose purpose is to let adaptive applications receive and transmit raw binary data without prior serialization.  
It distinguishes two stream styles: (1) a continuous untyped byte stream and (2) a datagram stream where header fields are typed and only the payload is raw.  
The interface is statically defined and protocol-specific; the document covers two Ethernet-based transports:

- IP-based (network layer): uses TCP or UDP sockets, supports unicast/multicast, allows TLS/IPSec protection and IAM access control; configuration is done at deployment.  
- IEEE 1722 (data-link layer): uses layer-2 MAC sockets with AAF, 61883_IIDC, RVF, CRF, TSCF, NTSCF subtypes, supports MAC unicast/multicast, exposes the 1722 header as typed data and the payload as raw bytes, and can be secured with MACsec and IAM policies; again, deployment-time attributes configure the connection.

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 “Raw Data Stream” specification.  
It contains:

- A local glossary (Table 2.1) that defines the acronyms and abbreviations used only in this document, covering networking terms (MAC, IP, TCP, UDP, TLS/DTLS, SOME/IP), end-to-end protection (E2E), and IEEE-1722 audio/video/control formats (61883_IIDC, AAF, RVF, CRF, TSCF, NTSCF).  
- Three normative definitions (§2.1.1 – §2.1.3):  
  – “MAC unicast socket connection” – layer-2 socket bound to a single MAC destination.  
  – “MAC multicast socket connection” – layer-2 socket bound to a multicast MAC destination.  
  – “AVTPDU-common-header” – the first 12 bits of an IEEE-1722 AVTP packet (subtype, header-specific bit, version).

nodes.title: MAC unicast socket connection :

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-header :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a terminology section from the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 “Raw Data Stream” specification.  
It first lists acronyms/abbreviations (MAC, IP, SOME/IP, TCP, UDP, E2E, TLS/DTLS, plus IEEE-1722 audio/video formats 61883_IIDC, AAF, RVF, CRF, TSCF, NTSCF).  
It then gives precise definitions for seven concepts:  
- MAC unicast socket connection (layer-2 socket bound to a single MAC destination)  
- MAC multicast socket connection (layer-2 socket bound to a multicast MAC)  
- AVTPDU-common-header (first 12 bits shared by all AVTP stream subtypes)  
- AVTPDU-common-stream-header (extension used by stream subtypes like 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, RVF)  
- AVTPDU-common-control-header (extension used by control subtypes like ADP)  
- AVTPDU-alternative-header (header variant for subtypes lacking the above commonalities, e.g. CRF, NTSCF)  
- AVTPDU-payload (the data portion following the header, e.g. audio samples in AAF)  
- Stream data producer (Ethernet end-node that continuously transmits a stream, synonymous with “talker”).

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header :
nodes.summary: The excerpt defines the structure of an AVTPDU (Audio-Video Transport Protocol Data Unit) as specified in IEEE 1722 and adopted by AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It introduces four header variants—AVTPDU-common-header, AVTPDU-common-stream-header, AVTPDU-common-control-header, and AVTPDU-alternative-header—each serving different subsets of AVTP stream subtypes (e.g., 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, RVF, ADP, CRF, NTSCF). It also describes the AVTPDU-payload as the data portion that follows the header and carries subtype-specific content (e.g., audio samples for AAF). Finally, it defines a “stream data producer” (synonymous with “talker”) as an Ethernet end-node that continuously generates stream data for one or more consumers.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-control-header :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt lists the IEEE-1722-based header and payload structures that can appear inside an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Raw Data Stream” AVTPDU:  
- AVTPDU-common-header – minimal fields present in every AVTPDU  
- AVTPDU-common-stream-header – extra fields for media sub-types such as 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, RVF  
- AVTPDU-common-control-header – extra fields for control sub-types such as ADP  
- AVTPDU-alternative-header – a divergent header used by sub-types like CRF and NTSCF that do not fit the two common patterns above  
- AVTPDU-payload – the variable-length data portion that follows the header and whose format is dictated by the subtype field in the header  
- Stream data producer (a.k.a. “talker”) – the Ethernet end-node that continuously generates the stream data that consumers receive

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header :
nodes.summary: The excerpt defines the structure of AVTPDU (Audio Video Transport Protocol Data Unit) headers used in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform raw data streams. It introduces four header variants—AVTPDU-common-header, AVTPDU-common-stream-header, AVTPDU-common-control-header, and AVTPDU-alternative-header—each serving specific AVTP stream subtypes (e.g., 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, RVF, ADP, CRF, NTSCF). It also specifies that the AVTPDU-payload follows the header and carries subtype-encoded data (e.g., audio samples for AAF). Finally, it defines a “stream data producer” (synonymous with “talker”) as an end node that continuously generates Ethernet stream data for one or more consumers.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-payload :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the structure of an AVTPDU (Audio Video Transport Protocol Data Unit) as specified in IEEE 1722 and adopted by AUTOSAR AP R25-11.  
It introduces four header variants:  
- AVTPDU-common-header – shared by every AVTP stream subtype.  
- AVTPDU-common-stream-header – extends the common header for media-oriented subtypes (61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, RVF).  
- AVTPDU-common-control-header – extends it for control-oriented subtypes (ADP).  
- AVTPDU-alternative-header – used when a subtype (CRF, NTSCF) does not fit the above patterns.  

The AVTPDU-payload is the second part of the packet and carries the actual subtype-encoded data (e.g., audio samples in an AAF payload).  
A “stream data producer” (equivalent to “talker”) is an end-node that continuously generates and transmits a stream to one or more receiving end-nodes.

nodes.title: Stream data producer :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the structure of an AVTPDU (Audio-Video Transport Protocol Data Unit) as specified in IEEE 1722 and adopted by AUTOSAR AP R25-11.  
It introduces four header variants—AVTPDU-common-header, AVTPDU-common-stream-header, AVTPDU-common-control-header, and AVTPDU-alternative-header—each serving different AVTP stream subtypes (e.g., 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, RVF, ADP, CRF, NTSCF).  
It also states that the AVTPDU-payload immediately follows the header and carries the actual subtype-specific data (e.g., audio samples for AAF).  
Finally, it defines a “stream data producer” (synonym “talker”) as an Ethernet end-node that continuously generates stream data consumed by one or more receivers.

nodes.title: Stream data consumer :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines key timing and synchronization concepts for AVB/TSN streaming in AUTOSAR AP.  
It introduces the “stream data consumer” (listener) as the endpoint that continuously receives Ethernet stream data.  
It then details the “AVTP presentation time” carried in the AVTPDU header: the gPTP instant when data is handed to the consumer’s time-sensitive application, computed as current global time plus “max transit time” and excluding local processing delays.  
“Max transit time” is the worst-case network delay from talker to listener, used to set this presentation offset.  
Finally, it specifies the “media clock” as a constant-rate hardware clock (e.g., 48 kHz) and the “media clock provider” as the node that distributes this clock to consumers via IEEE-1722 CRF streams containing correlated timestamps.

nodes.title: AVTP presentation time :
Max transit time :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines key timing and synchronization concepts for Ethernet-based streaming in AUTOSAR AP R25-11.  
It introduces:  
- Stream data consumer (listener) – the end node that continuously receives stream data.  
- AVTP presentation time – the gPTP instant, carried in the AVTPDU header, when data is delivered to the consumer, computed as current global time plus max transit time (excluding local processing).  
- Max transit time – worst-case network delay from talker to listener used to set this presentation offset.  
- Media clock – a constant-rate hardware clock (e.g., 48 kHz).  
- Media clock provider – the node that generates this clock and distributes it in an IEEE-1722 CRF stream to one or more media clock consumers.

nodes.title: Media clock :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines key timing and synchronization concepts for AVB/TSN streaming in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform.  
It explains that a stream data consumer (listener) is the end node that continuously receives Ethernet stream data.  
The AVTP presentation time, carried in the AVTPDU header as avtp_timestamp, tells the consumer when to hand the payload to its time-sensitive application; it equals current gPTP time plus the chosen Max transit time, excluding local processing or format-conversion delays.  
Max transit time is the worst-case network delay from talker to listener, used to set that presentation offset.  
A Media clock is a constant-rate hardware clock (e.g., 48 kHz) and is hosted by a Media clock provider node, which distributes the rate to consumers via IEEE-1722 CRF streams containing presentation timestamps.

nodes.title: Media clock provider :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines key timing and synchronization concepts for AVB/TSN streaming in AUTOSAR AP:

- Stream data consumer (listener) – the end-node that continuously receives the stream.  
- AVTP presentation time – the gPTP instant, carried in the AVTPDU header as avtp_timestamp, at which payload data is handed to the consumer; it equals current global time plus max transit time and excludes local processing/format-conversion delays.  
- Max transit time – worst-case network delay from talker to listener used to set this presentation offset.  
- Media clock – a constant-rate hardware clock (e.g., 48 kHz) that paces the media.  
- Media clock provider – the node that generates this clock and distributes it in an IEEE-1722 CRF stream to one or many media clock consumers.

nodes.title: Media clock consumer :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines five key terms from the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 specification:

1. Media clock consumer – an end-node that receives an IEEE1722 CRF stream and recovers its media clock from it.  
2. ACF-stream – an IEEE1722 TSCF/NTSCF stream that transports encapsulated bus frames as ACF-messages inside its AVTPDU payload.  
3. ACF-message – an IEEE1722-encapsulated bus frame (e.g., CAN) comprising an ACF-message-header and an ACF-message-payload; multiple messages can share one stream.  
4. ACF-message-header – the first 16 bits of an ACF-message: 7 bits for message type, 9 bits for payload length.  
5. ACF-message-payload – the data portion of an ACF-message, whose format matches the bus type indicated in the header (e.g., a CAN2.0 frame in an ACF_CAN payload).

nodes.title: ACF-stream :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines five key terms from the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 Raw-DataStream specification:

- Media clock consumer – an end-node that receives an IEEE1722 CRF stream and recovers its media clock from it.  
- ACF-stream – an IEEE1722 TSCF/NTSCF stream that carries encapsulated bus frames (ACF-messages) inside its AVTPDU payload.  
- ACF-message – the IEEE1722 encapsulation of a single bus frame (e.g., CAN), comprising an ACF-message-header and ACF-message-payload; multiple messages can share one stream.  
- ACF-message-header – the 16-bit prefix of an ACF-message: 7 bits for message type, 9 bits for payload length.  
- ACF-message-payload – the data portion of an ACF-message, whose content matches the type declared in the header (e.g., a CAN 2.0 frame).

nodes.title: ACF-message :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines five key terms from the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 Raw Data Stream specification:

- Media clock consumer – an end-node that receives an IEEE-1722 CRF stream from a media-clock provider and recovers its local media clock from it.  
- ACF-stream – an IEEE-1722 stream (TSCF or NTSCF subtype) that carries encapsulated bus frames as ACF-messages inside its AVTPDU payload.  
- ACF-message – the IEEE-1722 container for a single bus frame (e.g., CAN); it comprises an ACF-message-header and an ACF-message-payload; several different ACF-messages can share one ACF-stream.  
- ACF-message-header – the first 16 bits of an ACF-message: 7 bits give the ACF-message type (e.g., ACF_CAN, ACF_LIN), the remaining 9 bits give the payload length.  
- ACF-message-payload – the data field that follows the header and holds the actual bus frame encoded according to the declared ACF-message type.

nodes.title: ACF-message-header :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines five key terms from the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 specification for raw data streaming:

- Media clock consumer – an end-node that receives an IEEE1722 CRF stream from a media clock provider and recovers its own media clock from it.  
- ACF-stream – an IEEE1722 stream (TSCF or NTSCF subtype) that carries encapsulated bus frames as ACF-messages inside its AVTPDU payload.  
- ACF-message – an IEEE1722-defined container for a single bus frame (e.g., CAN) consisting of an ACF-message-header and an ACF-message-payload; multiple messages can share one stream.  
- ACF-message-header – the 16-bit lead field of an ACF-message: 7 bits for the message type (e.g., ACF_CAN, ACF_LIN) and 9 bits for the payload length.  
- ACF-message-payload – the data field that follows the header and carries the actual bus frame corresponding to the declared message type.

nodes.title: ACF-message-payload :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines key terms for handling raw data streams in AUTOSAR AP R25-11.  
It introduces the “media clock consumer” as an end node that recovers its media clock from an IEEE1722 CRF stream supplied by a media clock provider.  
It then specifies the “ACF-stream” as an IEEE1722 TSCF/NTSCF stream that carries encapsulated bus frames.  
An “ACF-message” is the encapsulated bus frame (e.g., CAN) composed of an “ACF-message-header” (7-bit type + 9-bit payload length) and an “ACF-message-payload” that carries the actual bus data; multiple such messages can share one ACF-stream.

structure.title: Constraints and assumptions
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the main topics or key points discussed within the provided excerpt.

nodes.title: Known limitations :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt lists the current constraints and assumptions for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Raw Data Stream feature:  
- Static, same-ECU configuration only—no dynamic service discovery or runtime topology changes.  
- No built-in safety protection; E2E safety must be handled at the application level.  
- Over IP/Ethernet: multicast is UDP-only, one-to-many outbound, unicast inbound; TCP is strictly 1-to-1.  
- Over IEEE 1722: only the AAF, 61883_IIDC, RVF, CRF, TSCF and NTSCF sub-types are supported; others may be added later.

structure.title: Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the dependencies of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (R25-11). It emphasizes that AUTOSAR does not standardize internal interfaces between functional clusters to allow flexibility and efficiency in implementation. The chapter serves as an informative guide for interactions between clusters without defining syntactical details, ensuring compatibility and supporting parallel development. It explicitly states that this functional cluster provides no public interfaces to others (Table 5.1) and lists required interfaces from other clusters, though the specific required interfaces are not detailed in the excerpt. Internal implementation details are left to the platform provider.

nodes.title: Provided Interfaces :
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains that the Raw Data Stream functional cluster has no standardized public interfaces that it provides to other functional clusters, and it lists none; it merely outlines the chapter structure for documenting any required interfaces from other clusters, while emphasizing that internal inter-cluster interfaces are deliberately left non-standardized to permit vendor-specific optimizations.

nodes.title: Required Interfaces :
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the dependencies of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (AP) R25-11. It explains that internal interfaces between functional clusters are not standardized to allow flexibility in implementation. The Raw Data Stream functional cluster does not provide any interfaces to other clusters but requires interfaces from three others: Cryptography (CryptoStack) for encrypted connections, Log and Trace (Logger) for standardized logging, and Time Synchronization (SynchronizedTimeBaseConsumer) for obtaining synchronized global time, particularly for IEEE1722-based communication.

structure.title: Requirements Tracing
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a requirements-traceability matrix for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Raw Data Stream” specification (R25-11).  
It maps every upstream requirement drawn from three source documents ([4] General Adaptive Platform requirements, [5] Communication Management requirements, [7] IEEE-1722 requirements) to the specific low-level software requirements (SWS_RDS_xxxx) that satisfy them.  
Key areas traced are:  
- Raw binary stream I/O API without datatype information  
- Transport independence (TCP/IP sockets offered, but API remains generic)  
- Native support for IEEE-1722 AVBTP streams  
- Secure-channel configuration and transmission  
- Platform-wide coding rules (return values, method/parameter/type names, use of ara::core types, avoidance of dynamic memory, special-member-function availability, non-copyable InstanceSpecifier-based classes, IAM and mapping violations).  
Empty cells in the “Satisfied by” column indicate requirements not addressed by this specification.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming using IP based protocols (network layer) :
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the functional specification of the Raw Data Streaming interface in AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It states that all interface operations are synchronous and blocking by default, with configurable timeout handling. Configuration is performed via RawDataStreamMapping and EthernetRawDataStreamMapping elements defined in TPS_ManifestSpecification. Security—TLS, IPSec, and IAM-based access control—is fully configurable in the deployment model. Applications can act as either client or server, and Figure 7.1 illustrates the logical view of RawDataStream usage.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming Interface :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines the functional specification of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Raw Data Stream” feature.  
Key points:  
- Synchronous, blocking-by-default API with per-operation timeout parameters.  
- Configuration via RawDataStreamMapping / EthernetRawDataStreamMapping elements defined in TPS_ManifestSpecification.  
- Optional security: TLS, IPSec, and IAM access control, all set in the deployment/mapping model.

nodes.title: Use cases :
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the RawDataStream interface use cases defined in AUTOSAR AP R25-11: it can act as a client connecting to, or a server waiting for, an external non-AUTOSAR sensor that delivers raw data over UDP or TCP, unicast or multicast sockets, and can likewise serve as client or server to another AUTOSAR RawDataStream instance. It notes that the supported scenarios are depicted in Figure 7.2 and that Ethernet socket configuration details for each case are provided in Chapter 10.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “RawDataStream” abstraction for IP-based raw data exchange.  
It specifies:

- Creation: a RawDataStream object is constructed from EthernetRawDataStreamClient/Server mapping artifacts, allocating the underlying socket.  
- Client-side API: Connect (TCP only), Shutdown, ReadData, WriteData.  
- Server-side API: WaitForConnection (TCP only), Shutdown, ReadData, WriteData; UDP variants of Connect/WaitForConnection are no-ops.  
- Shutdown is mandatory; if skipped, the destructor performs it implicitly.  
- ReadData/WriteData transfer byte buffers; WriteData accepts an optional timeout and only guarantees TCP ordering.  
- All blocking operations (Connect, WaitForConnection, Read, Write) support an optional runtime timeout; expiry returns RdsErrc::kCommunicationTimeout and rolls back state.  
- Security: IAM enforces that only processes granted “ara::com::rawdatastream” permission may instantiate RawDataStreamClient or RawDataStreamServer.

nodes.title: Security :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies the core I/O behaviour of the AUTOSAR Adaptive “RawDataStream” component:  
- ReadData pulls a requested byte count from the stream into a caller-supplied buffer and returns the amount actually copied.  
- WriteData pushes a caller-supplied byte buffer to the socket; it returns the count written, supports an optional timeout, and relies on TCP/UDP transport (TCP preserves order, UDP does not).  
- All Connect, WaitForConnection, Read and Write operations can be given a runtime timeout; if the timeout fires the call fails with RdsErrc::kCommunicationTimeout and the stream state is rolled back to its pre-call condition.  
- Creation of either a RawDataStreamClient or RawDataStreamServer object is gated by IAM-based access-control, enforcing process-level authorization before any stream can be instantiated.

nodes.title: Access Control via IAM :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies the core I/O behaviour of the AUTOSAR Adaptive “RawDataStream” component (release R25-11) and its security rules.

Main points:
- ReadData/WriteData operations transfer arbitrary byte blocks; WriteData may carry a timeout, returns actual count, and (for TCP) only enqueues data without waiting for bus transmission.
- All connect, read, write and wait calls accept an optional runtime timeout; if it expires the call fails with kCommunicationTimeout and the stream state is rolled back.
- Access is controlled through IAM: creating a client or server with an InstanceSpecifier that is not mapped to the calling Process in the model is rejected as a violation.
- Traffic can be secured via TLS 1.2, DTLS 1.2 or IPSec; secure UDP/TCP channels are created automatically when TlsSecureComProps are present in EthernetRawDataStreamMapping, and all subsequent communication uses that channel.
- Recommended cipher suites for (D)TLS are TLS_PSK_WITH_NULL_SHA256 (authenticity/integrity) and TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (confidentiality).

nodes.title: Secure Communication :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies how AUTOSAR AP R25-11 handles RawDataStream access violations and details the security model for raw-data streaming. It mandates that an InstanceSpecifier must point to a mapped RPortPrototype for the requesting Process; otherwise it is a violation. It then lists the supported transport (TCP/UDP) and security protocols (TLS 1.2, DTLS 1.2, IPSec). Two upstream-driven requirements are quoted: the stack must create secure UDP/TCP channels for every TlsSecureComProps entry in EthernetRawDataStreamMapping, and all RawDataStream traffic must use the corresponding secure channel tied to an EthernetCommunicationConnector. Finally, it states that (D)TLS channels can provide authenticity, integrity and confidentiality, and it names two required cipher suites—TLS_PSK_WITH_NULL_SHA256 and TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256—for authenticated and confidential streaming respectively.

nodes.title: Creation and use of secure channels for Raw Data Stream- ing :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies how the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 Raw Data Stream handles mis-configured InstanceSpecifiers (treating them as violations), mandates that TCP/UDP traffic be protected through TLS 1.2, DTLS 1.2 or IPSec, requires creation and exclusive use of secure UDP/TCP channels defined by TlsSecureComProps in EthernetRawDataStreamMapping, and lists the PSK-based cipher suites (TLS_PSK_WITH_NULL_SHA256 for authenticity, TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 for confidentiality) that the (D)TLS implementation should support.

nodes.title: (D)TLS for Raw Data Streaming :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies security rules for AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Raw Data Streams: it mandates TLS/DTLS/IPsec protection for all TCP/UDP traffic, lists required cipher-suites, defines when each secure channel (TLS over TCP, DTLS over UDP, IPsec) must be created based on EthernetRawDataStreamMapping configuration, and describes how a RawDataStreamClient instance is created with an InstanceSpecifier.

nodes.title: Raw Data Stream Interfaces :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies security requirements for AUTOSAR AP R25-11 Raw Data Streams: it mandates TLS for TCP-based streams, DTLS for UDP-based streams, and IPsec for node-to-node protection, all configured via EthernetRawDataStreamMapping. It also defines that a client-side RawDataStream instance is created through the exception-less RawDataStreamClient::Create() call, receiving a network-binding Specifier.

nodes.title: Creation of a RawDataStream :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how AUTOSAR AP R25-11 secures and instantiates raw-data streams:

Security – three mutually exclusive secure channels are mandated:  
- TLS over TCP ([SWS_RDS_90213])  
- DTLS over UDP ([SWS_RDS_90214])  
- IPsec between nodes ([SWS_RDS_90215])  

Instantiation – for every stream two complementary objects are created:  
- RawDataStreamClient ([SWS_RDS_10511]) and RawDataStreamServer ([SWS_RDS_10512]) via exception-less Create() constructors that parse manifest credentials, open/bind sockets, join multicast groups if required, and store endpoint handles.  

Connection – only the client side performs an explicit Connect() ([SWS_RDS_10513]) which establishes a TCP connection; UDP remains connection-less while still enforcing address-restricted packet exchange.

nodes.title: Set up a RawDataStream connection :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “RawDataStream” objects are created, connected, and torn down for both 1:1 (unicast) and 1:N (multicast) communication over UDP or TCP.  
Key points:

- Constructors of RawDataStreamClient and RawDataStreamServer instantiate the correct socket type (unicast TCP/UDP or multicast UDP) according to the credentials supplied, bind/join the address/port, and store the endpoint handle.  
- Client::Connect() establishes a TCP connection (no-op for UDP); Server::WaitForConnection() puts a TCP socket into listen state (no-op for UDP).  
- Shutdown() on either side closes the full-duplex TCP connection or simply closes the UDP socket.  
- ReadData() on the client reads from the multicast socket if multicast credentials exist (1:N), otherwise from the connected unicast socket (1:1).

nodes.title: Shutdown of a RawDataStream connection :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies the lifecycle operations of AUTOSAR AP RawDataStream sockets:  
- TLS/DTLS initialization when security is configured  
- Server-side WaitForConnection() behavior—TCP listens/accepts, UDP no-op  
- Client and Server ShutDown()—graceful, full-duplex TCP close before socket release  
- Client ReadData()—reads from multicast socket if credentials present, otherwise from unicast TCP connection

nodes.title: Read data from a RawDataStream connection :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies the life-cycle and I/O behaviour of the AUTOSAR AP RawDataStream (RDS) abstraction over TCP/UDP sockets:

- **Connection set-up**:  
  – TLS/DTLS is initialised if security is configured.  
  – `RawDataStreamServer::WaitForConnection()` puts a TCP socket into listen/accept mode; for UDP it is a no-op.

- **Connection tear-down**:  
  – `RawDataStreamClient::ShutDown()` and `RawDataStreamServer::ShutDown()` perform a full-duplex shutdown of the TCP socket (or simply close the UDP socket) so that neither sending nor receiving is possible afterwards.

- **Reading data**:  
  – `RawDataStreamClient::ReadData()` reads from the multicast socket if multicast credentials exist, otherwise from the unicast TCP/UDP socket created earlier.  
  – `RawDataStreamServer::ReadData()` always reads from the unicast socket.  
  – Both use zero-copy (`std::unique_ptr`) to transfer ownership of the received buffer to the caller.

- **Writing data**:  
  – `RawDataStreamClient::WriteData()` writes to the unicast socket; if the client has multicast credentials the same socket may be shared for multicast RX and unicast TX.  
  – `RawDataStreamServer::WriteData()` writes to the multicast socket when remote multicast credentials are present, otherwise to the unicast TCP/UDP socket established during connection set-up.  
  – Again, zero-copy semantics are applied for the outgoing buffer.

All items trace back to upstream requirements RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, RS_CM_00412.

nodes.title: Write data to a RawDataStream connection :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “RawDataStream” service performs zero-copy read and write operations over TCP/UDP sockets.  
Key points:

- ReadData() on RawDataStreamServer and RawDataStreamClient pulls bytes from the established unicast socket and hands ownership to the caller via std::unique_ptr.  
- WriteData() on either class pushes bytes to the socket chosen at construction: multicast if Remote Multicast Credentials exist (1:N), otherwise the connected TCP or the created UDP unicast socket (1:1).  
- A single socket may be shared for multicast reception plus unicast transmission in 1:N UDP + 1:1 UDP control scenarios.  
- All transfers use zero-copy semantics for efficiency.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming using IEEE1722 protocol (data link layer) :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform exposes a synchronous, blocking “Raw Data Streaming” interface that carries payload over IEEE-1722 data-link-layer streams.  
Configuration is done entirely in the deployment/manifest by mapping each logical stream to Ethernet socket parameters (RawDataStreamMapping / EthernetMacRawDataStreamMapping); security is added optionally through MACSec and IAM rules, also declared in the same manifest.  
An application can act as either an IEEE-1722 consumer or producer; the logical view (Fig. 7.3) illustrates these two roles connected through the platform’s Raw Data Streaming instances.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming Interface :
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains that the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 Raw Data Streaming interface uses the IEEE1722 protocol at the data-link layer, performs synchronous/blocking calls, and is configured through RawDataStreamMapping and EthernetMacRawDataStreamMapping parameters defined in TPS_ManifestSpecification. Security is provided by optional MACSec and IAM mechanisms, also configured in the deployment model. Applications can act as IEEE1722 stream producers or consumers, and Figure 7.3 illustrates the logical view of these streaming instances.

nodes.title: Use cases :
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 Raw Data Stream specification for IEEE-1722: it lists four supported 1:1, 1:n, n:1 and n:m stream use-cases, states that streaming is stateless and unidirectional between statically configured producer/consumer classes, and requires applications to instantiate the provided IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer or IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer templates.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform “Raw Data Stream” interface for IEEE-1722 AVB/TSN traffic.  
It lists the four supported streaming topologies (1:1, 1:n, n:1, n:m) and states that every IEEE-1722 stream is statically configured in the Service Instance Manifest, is stateless and unidirectional, and is accessed through C++ template classes IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer/Producer instantiated for one of six allowed IEEE-1722 sub-types.  
It details how the InstanceSpecifier links manifest entries to a created data-link-layer socket, how socket options, VLAN priority, destination MAC and MTU are taken from the deployment model, and how header fields are composed on transmit and validated on receive.  
Finally it specifies the three runtime operations (Connect, Shutdown, ReadData/WriteData), their error returns if already connected/disconnected, automatic shutdown on destruction, and that access is gated by the AUTOSAR IAM policy.

nodes.title: Security :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the behavior of IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer/Consumer instances when establishing or tearing down a local AVTP socket connection:  
- Connect is idempotent—if already connected it returns kStreamAlreadyConnected.  
- Shutdown explicitly requests disconnection; if already disconnected it returns kStreamNotConnected.  
- Destructors automatically invoke Shutdown if the socket is still connected.  
- IAM-based access control (requirement RS_AP_00158) is mentioned but not detailed.

nodes.title: Access Control via IAM :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines the life-cycle, error-handling and security rules for AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform IEEE-1722 Raw-Data streaming.  
It mandates:  
- Connect/Shutdown idempotency (return kStreamAlreadyConnected / kStreamNotConnected if already in the requested state)  
- automatic internal Shutdown on destructor if still connected  
- IAM-based access control: a process may create a Consumer/Producer only for portPrototype entries mapped to it in the RawDataStreamMapping  
- optional MACSec protection on the data-link layer (outside the Raw-Data-Stream cluster)  
- ReadData semantics: the consumer reads at most maxNumberOfDatagrams, returns only datagrams that pass AVTPDU-header inspection, delivered as ara::core::Vector of IEEE-1722 subtype.

nodes.title: Secure Communication :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies rules for creating IEEE1722 raw-data stream consumers/producers, mandates that only port prototypes mapped to the calling process may be used (else it is a violation), notes that MACSec can secure the link but is out-of-scope, and defines the ReadData operation: it reads at most maxNumberOfDatagrams, returns only datagrams whose AVTPDU header passes the configured consistency check, and delivers them in an ara::core::Vector of IEEE1722 subtype.

nodes.title: Raw Data Streaming Interfaces :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies rules for creating IEEE1722 raw-data stream consumers/producers, mandates that only mapped port prototypes may be instantiated, notes that MACSec may secure the link but is outside the cluster’s scope, and details the ReadData interface: it returns at most maxNumberOfDatagrams after successful AVTPDU-header inspection, with results delivered as an ara::core::Vector of IEEE1722 sub-type.

nodes.title: Consume IEEE1722 stream data :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies rules for creating IEEE1722 raw-data-stream consumer/producer instances: supplying an InstanceSpecifier that does not map to the correct RPortPrototype is a violation. It notes that MACSec can secure the link-layer traffic but is outside the RDS cluster’s scope. It then defines the Consume interface: ReadData shall return at most maxNumberOfDatagrams, only after successful AVTPDU-header inspection; valid datagrams are delivered in an ara::core::Vector of the appropriate IEEE1722 subtype, and header inspection checks received AVTPDU fields against the consumer’s configuration.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-header inspection :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies rules for creating and using IEEE1722 raw-data stream consumers/producers in AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It mandates that constructors must receive an InstanceSpecifier referencing a RawDataStreamMapping for the calling process; otherwise it is a violation. Security may be added via MACSec but is out-of-scope. For consumption, the ReadData call returns only datagrams that pass AVTPDU-header inspection: subtype, version, timestamp, sequence-number, and stream-ID must match configuration; mismatches log errors and discard datagrams. For production, an IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer is instantiated from manifest data; the application supplies an ara::core::Vector of correctly typed datagrams—mismatched ones are skipped.

nodes.title: Produce IEEE1722 stream data :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines how AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform handles IEEE-1722 AVTP raw-data streams.  
It specifies:

- Mandatory AVTPDU-header inspection rules for incoming streams (CRF/NTSCF subtypes with common-stream-header) – sequence-number continuity and exact match of the 64-bit stream-id against the consumer’s configured value; mismatches are logged but processing continues for sequence errors and is aborted for stream-id errors.

- Producer-side API: applications create an `IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer` instance templated on the desired 1722 subtype; they supply a vector of per-datagram objects containing all header fields and payload.  
  `WriteData` iterates over this vector, validates every mandatory and optional header field (range, presence, default/calculation fallback), skips invalid datagrams with log entries, builds the complete 1722 header + payload, transmits the datagram on the socket, and finally returns the count of successfully sent datagrams.

- Shared header-field handling: both consumer and producer maintain per-stream state (e.g., sequence number) for streams using the AVTP common-stream-header format.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-header creation :
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains how an IEEE1722 (AVTP) datagram is built and sent: after validating the header fields, the stack forms an IEEE1722 header of the configured subtype, appends the application payload, transmits the frame on the assigned socket and returns the transmit count. It then details the “AVTPDU-header creation” procedure: on every WriteData call the producer checks the application-supplied header values against its configuration, composes the header according to IEEE-1722 (using AVTPDU-common-header, ‑common-stream-header or ‑alternative-header formats, but not the control-header), and handles shared fields such as the sequence number, which each stream instance maintains independently.

nodes.title: Treatment of shared AVTPDU-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt details the AUTOSAR AP “Raw Data Stream” specification rules for building and sending an IEEE-1722 (AVTP) datagram.  
Key points:

- After validating the incoming IEEE-1722 header fields, the stack constructs a new IEEE-1722 header of the configured subtype, appends the user payload, transmits the datagram on the assigned socket and returns the number of sent frames.  
- Header creation is triggered by the application’s WriteData call on an IEEE-1722RawDataStreamProducer instance; it first checks that the application-supplied AVTPDU-header field values are consistent with the producer’s static configuration.  
- Only three of the four IEEE-1722 AVTPDU header formats are supported: common-header, common-stream-header and alternative-header; the common-control-header is excluded.  
- Shared header fields (sequence number, stream ID, time-uncertain flag) are handled in dedicated sub-clauses:  
  – Sequence number: maintained per stream, incremented mod-256.  
  – Stream ID: application-supplied MAC/UID used if valid, otherwise the configured values from the consumer/producer mapping are taken.  
  – Time-uncertain (tu) bit: set to 1 if the referenced GlobalTimeDomain is not synchronized, else 0.  
- MTU enforcement: if header + payload exceed the socket MTU, header creation is aborted.  
- Subtype field in the common-header is set automatically: 0x04 for CRF, 0x02 for AAF, 0x00 for IEC-61883/IIDC streams.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (R25-11) builds the IEEE-1722 AVTPDU header for a Raw Data Stream.  
Key points:

- **Time-uncertain (tu) bit**: set to 1 if the referenced GlobalTimeDomain is not synchronized, otherwise 0.  
- **MTU check**: if the combined length of AVTPDU-header and payload exceeds the socket MTU, header creation is aborted.  
- **Common-header fields**:  
  – *Subtype*: choose 0x00, 0x02, 0x04, 0x05, 0x07 or 0x82₁₆ according to whether the stream is IEC61883-IIDC, AAF, CRF, TSCF, RVF or NTSCF.  
  – *Version*: copy the configured value assigned to the stream.  
- **Stream-specific header fields**:  
  – *Media-clock restart (mr)*: use application-supplied value if valid, else 0.  
  – *Stream-ID valid (sv)*: always set to 1.

nodes.title: AVTPDU-common-stream-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a detailed, normative section of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (R25-11) “RawDataStream” specification that prescribes, rule-by-rule, how every relevant field in an outgoing IEEE 1722 AVTPDU header must be populated when the stack creates a packet.  
It first maps the three allowed stream-format configurations (RVF, NTSCF, TSCF) to their fixed AVTP subtype values (0x07, 0x82, 0x05).  
For the common AVTPDU header it mandates:  
- version must be copied from the configured value.  
For the common-stream-header it mandates:  
- mr (media-clock-restart) = application-provided value if valid, else 0  
- sv (stream-id-valid) = 1  
- sequence_num = current counter value  
- tu (timestamp-uncertain) = value determined by [SWS_RDS_10548]  
- stream_id = constructed per [SWS_RDS_10547]  
- avtp_timestamp = application-provided value if valid, otherwise presentation time calculated as synchronized global time plus maxTransitTime taken from the active IEEE1722Tp connection object  
- stream_data_length = byte length of the current datagram instance.  

A short subsection notes that any “alternative-header” fields are subtype-specific and covered later.  

Finally, for 61883_IIDC subtype streams with tag=0, it lists the exact constant or configured values that must be written into the 61883-specific header fields (gv, gateway_info, tag, channel, tcode).

nodes.title: AVTPDU-alternative-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt details how to populate specific header fields when building IEEE-1722 AVTPDUs within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Raw-Data-Stream stack.  
It mandates:

- For every AVTPDU carrying an IEEE-1722 stream, the “stream_data_length” field in the AVTP common-stream-header must be set to the byte length supplied by the corresponding datagram instance (e.g., IEEE1722DatagramAAF).  
- When the AVTP stream-data subtype is 61883_IIDC and the producer’s configured tag field equals 0, the implementation must additionally write the 61883_IIDC-specific header fields: gv=0, gateway_info=0, tag=producer-configured value, channel=producer-configured value, tcode=producer-configured value.

nodes.title: 61883_IIDC-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a draft specification (AUTOSAR AP R25-11) that details how software shall fill the IEEE-1722 AVTPDU header fields when transmitting raw-data streams.  
Main points:

- **Stream-data-length rule**: For any IEEE-1722 stream using the AVTPDU common-stream-header, the “stream_data_length” field must be set to the byte length carried in the outgoing datagram instance.  
- **Subtype-specific headers**: Separate rules are given for the 61883_IIDC subtype; other subtypes (CRF, NTSCF) are only mentioned as having their own chapters.  
- **61883_IIDC header construction – tag = 0**:  
  – Set gv=0, gateway_info=0, tag/channel/tcode/sy to the configured values from the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance.  
- **61883_IIDC header construction – tag = 1 (additional CIP header)**:  
  – Add qi_1=00₂, SID=63₁₀, DBS/FN/QPC/SPH/DBC/FMT to configured or application-provided values (QPC/DBC default to 0 if absent/invalid), qi_2=10₂.  
- **61883_IIDC – tag=1 & SPH=0**:  
  – Set tv from metadata (else 0), avtp_timestamp per [SWS_RDS_10557], FDF from app (else 0), SYT=FFFF₁₆, and place payload in cip_no_sph_payload.  
- **61883_IIDC – tag=1 & SPH=1**:  
  – tv=0, FDF from app (else 0), payload goes in cip_with_sph_payload (which already contains the source-packet header timestamp and may hold multiple packets).  
- **General notes**: AUTOSAR does not support AVTP gateway functionality; all requirements trace back to upstream requirement RS_CM_00413.

nodes.title: AAF-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform shall populate the AVTPDU header fields for IEEE-1722 streams that use the AAF (Audio Format) subtype.  
It lists the exact header fields that must be set—format, sp, evt for every AAF stream, plus nsr/channels_per_frame/bit_depth/pcm_data_payload when the format is PCM, or nfr/streams_per_frame/aes3_data_type_*/aes3_dt_ref when the format is AES3—and states that each value is taken from the corresponding configuration attribute of the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance.  
A short note adds that ACF (Control Format) subtype headers are also covered, although SRP is not supported and the sv bit is hard-coded to 1.

nodes.title: ACF-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how the AUTOSAR Raw-Data Stream functional cluster fills the AVTPDU headers for IEEE-1722 streams:

- AAF (PCM-Audio) subtype: copy the application-supplied PCM payload and set the nfr, streams_per_frame, aes3_data_type_h, aes3_dt_ref and aes3_data_type_l fields from the configured IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer attributes.

- ACF subtypes (NTSCF/TSCF): copy the application-supplied ACF-message payload, set sv = 1, and for NTSCF fill ntscf_data_length with the accumulated ACF-message length, while for TSCF fill stream_data_length with the payload byte count.

nodes.title: CRF-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies, for AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, the exact values that must be written into the AVTPDU header fields when the stream data subtype is CRF (Clock Reference Format) or RVF (Raw Video Format).  
For CRF it lists every dedicated CRF header field (sv, mr, fs, tu, sequence_num, type, stream_id, pull, base_frequency, crf_data_length, timestamp_interval, crf_data) and states whether each is taken from application input, derived from other SWS rules, or copied from the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer configuration, with default values when data are invalid.  
For RVF it gives the same style of prescription for the RVF-specific header fields (active_pixels, total_lines, ap, f, ef, evt, pd, i), again indicating application-supplied vs. configured vs. default-0 sources.  
Both sub-clauses reference the relevant IEEE 1722 chapters and note that the remaining payload-level fields are outside the Raw Data Stream cluster’s scope.

nodes.title: RVF-header fields :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt details how the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform (R25-11) Raw-Data-Stream functional cluster builds IEEE 1722 AVTP Data Units whose stream-data subtype is “RVF” (Raw Video Format).  
It lists the exact RVF-specific header fields that must be populated—either from application-supplied values or from pre-configured attributes of the IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer instance—and states that missing/invalid application values default to 0.  
It also specifies two error-handling rules for the ReadData/WriteData operations: if the underlying socket is disconnected the call returns kStreamAlreadyDisconnected; if the operation is interrupted by a system signal it returns kInterruptedBySignal after cleaning up internal state.

nodes.title: Error handling for consuming or producing IEEE1722 stream data :
nodes.summary: The excerpt details how an IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer populates the pixel_depth, pixel_format, frame_rate, colorspace, num_lines, i_seq_num and line_number fields of an AVTPDU, with the first four taken from configured values and the last three supplied by the application (defaulting to 0 if missing/invalid). It then specifies two error-handling rules for the ReadData/WriteData operations of IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer/Producer: return kStreamAlreadyDisconnected if the socket is disconnected, and return kInterruptedBySignal (after resetting states and releasing resources) if the operation is interrupted by a system signal.

nodes.title: Functional cluster lifecycle :
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the lifecycle and reporting behavior of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster in AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It states that no special startup or shutdown handling is required because the cluster maintains no persistent state. It also declares that the cluster defines no security events and no non-verbose (modelled DLT) log messages. The only reporting item provided is a single DLT violation message ([SWS_CORE_13003]) that signals an integrity violation when an InstanceSpecifier cannot be resolved or points to a non-PortPrototype element; the message carries the offending process’s meta-model identifier.

nodes.title: Startup :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies the lifecycle and reporting behavior of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster in AUTOSAR AP R25-11: it requires no special startup or shutdown handling and maintains no persistent state; it defines no security events or non-verbose log messages; the only modeled message is a DLT fatal-level integrity-violation message (ID 0x80001ffc) emitted when an InstanceSpecifier is unresolvable or mis-references a non-PortPrototype element, carrying the offending process’s UTF-8 encoded short-name path.

nodes.title: Shutdown :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies the Raw Data Stream functional cluster’s lifecycle behavior and reporting rules: no special startup/shutdown actions or persistent state, no security events, no regular DLT log messages, and only one mandatory DLT fatal message (ID 0x80001ffc) for model-integrity violations involving an unresolvable or mis-typed InstanceSpecifier, accompanied by the offending process identifier.

nodes.title: Reporting :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies the lifecycle, reporting, and diagnostic behavior of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster in AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It states that no special startup or shutdown handling is required because the cluster maintains no persistent state. It explicitly declares that the cluster defines no security events and no non-verbose (modelled DLT) log messages. The only reporting item provided is a mandatory DLT violation message ([SWS_CORE_13003]) that is emitted when an InstanceSpecifier cannot be resolved or points to a non-PortPrototype element; the message ID, type, and argument format for this violation are given.

nodes.title: Security Events :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies the lifecycle and reporting behavior of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster in AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It states that no special startup or shutdown handling is required because the cluster maintains no persistent state. It also clarifies that the cluster defines no security events and no non-verbose (modelled DLT) log messages. The only reporting item provided is a single violation message ([SWS_CORE_13003]) that logs a fatal DLT message when an InstanceSpecifier cannot be resolved or refers to a non-PortPrototype element, including the offending process’s meta-model identifier.

nodes.title: Log Messages :
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the lifecycle and reporting behavior of the Raw Data Stream functional cluster in AUTOSAR AP R25-11. It states that no special startup or shutdown handling is required because the cluster maintains no state across power cycles. It also clarifies that the cluster defines no security events and no non-verbose (modelled DLT) log messages. Finally, it provides the detailed specification of a single violation message ([SWS_CORE_13003]) for integrity violations involving InstanceSpecifier resolution, including its ID, type, and argument structure.

nodes.title: Violation Messages :
nodes.summary: The excerpt specifies the Raw Data Stream functional cluster’s life-cycle and reporting behavior in AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11. It states that no special startup or shutdown handling is required and that the cluster maintains no state across power cycles. It explicitly declares that the cluster defines no security events, no non-verbose DLT log messages, and no production errors. It then details four mandatory DLT fatal-level violation messages (InstanceSpecifierMappingIntegrityViolation, PortInterfaceMappingViolation, ProcessMappingViolation, InstanceSpecifierAlreadyInUseViolation) including their IDs, descriptions, and argument structures.

nodes.title: Production Errrors :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 RawDataStream functional cluster.  
It defines a fatal log message (0x80001ff9) that reports a modeling violation, containing five UTF-8 string fields: modeledProcess, location, instanceSpecifier, className, and an unnamed uint8.  
It states that the cluster defines no production errors (Diagnostic Events).  
It then introduces the API specification chapter, detailing the structure of API tables (kind, port interfaces, header files, scope, symbol, thread safety, syntax, template/parameter/return/exception/error information) and explains how PortInterfaces are bound to C++ API classes via constructing functions that accept an ara::core::InstanceSpecifier.

structure.title: API specification
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 “Raw Data Stream” software specification.  
It introduces the API-reference chapter and explains the column headings used in every API table (Kind, Port Interfaces, Header File, Scope, Symbol, Thread Safety, Syntax, Template/Parameters, Return, Exception Safety, Exceptions, Violations, Errors, Description).  
It then presents Table 8.1, which maps each PortInterface defined by the functional cluster to its corresponding C++ API class and lists the constructor-like functions (special-member, named, or factory) that accept an ara::core::InstanceSpecifier to instantiate that class.

nodes.title: PortInterface to API class binding :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 “Raw Data Stream” software specification.  
It introduces the API reference section, explains the column headings used in every API table (Kind, Port Interfaces, Header File, Thread Safety, Syntax, Parameters, Return, Exceptions, Errors, Description, etc.), and then supplies the first concrete API tables:  

- Table 8.2 lists the PortInterface-to-API-class/function bindings for the functional cluster, giving the C++ classes `ara::rds::IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer`, `IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer`, `RawDataStreamClient`, `RawDataStreamServer` and their static `Create` factory functions.  

- Section 8.2.1 immediately follows with the full API table for the `RawDataStreamClient` class, specifying its namespace, header files, purpose (“reading and writing binary data streams over a network connection”), and its formal C++ declaration.

nodes.title: Header: ara/rds/raw_data_stream.h :
nodes.summary:
The fragment is a formal AUTOSAR specification excerpt that lists the API classes and factory “Create” functions for the Raw Data Stream (RDS) service in Adaptive Platform R25-11.  
It introduces:

- IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer / Producer classes and their Create functions for AVB/1722 traffic  
- RawDataStreamClient and RawDataStreamServer classes and their Create functions for generic binary-stream I/O  
- The binding of these classes to the PortInterface sub-class  
- The location of the public header ara/rds/raw_data_stream.h and the forward-declaration header ara/rds/rds_fwd.h  
- A concise description of RawDataStreamClient as a final class that enables reading and writing of raw binary data streams over a network connection

nodes.title: Class: RawDataStreamClient :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR AP R25-11 “RawDataStream” software specification.  
It lists the formal API definitions that bind the PortInterface sub-classes to their implementing C++ classes and factory functions:

- IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer / IEEE1722RawDataStreamConsumer::Create  
- IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer / IEEE1722RawDataStreamProducer::Create  
- RawDataStreamClient / RawDataStreamClient::Create  
- RawDataStreamServer / RawDataStreamServer::Create  

A detailed specification of the RawDataStreamClient class is then provided: it is a final class in namespace ara::rds, exposed through headers ara/rds/raw_data_stream.h and ara/rds/rds_fwd.h, implementing the RawDataStreamClientInterface, and intended for reading and writing binary data streams over a network connection.

nodes.title: Public Member Functions :
nodes.summary: The excerpt defines the copy and move constructors of the ara::rds::RawDataStreamClient class: the copy constructor is explicitly deleted (forbidden), while the move constructor is provided with noexcept guarantee, accepts an rvalue-reference to another RawDataStreamClient, is exception-safe but not thread-safe, and both are governed by upstream requirements RS_CM_00410–RS_CM_00412, RS_AP_00145, RS_AP_00147.

nodes.title: Special Member Functions :
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the main topics and key points discussed within its content.

nodes.title: Copy Constructor :
nodes.summary: The partial document specifies the copy and move constructors of the ara::rds::RawDataStreamClient class: the copy constructor is explicitly deleted (forbidden), while the move constructor is provided with noexcept guarantee, takes an rvalue-reference parameter, is exception-safe but not thread-safe, and is governed by upstream requirements RS_CM_00410–RS_CM_00412 and RS_AP_00145/00147.

nodes.title: Move Constructor :
nodes.summary:
The excerpt lists the four special-member-function signatures of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform class ara::rds::RawDataStreamClient:  
- Copy constructor and copy-assignment operator are explicitly deleted, forbidding duplication.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.summary:
The excerpt details the configuration model for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Raw Data Stream (RDS) functional cluster.  
It specifies how Ethernet socket connections for RawDataStreamClients and RawDataStreamServers are statically declared in the Deployment model’s Service Instance Manifest, listing every configurable endpoint/credential element (local IP, UDP/TCP ports, socket options, (D)TLS properties, unicast & multicast credentials).  
It then gives the run-time rules: clients must supply remote unicast credentials, may optionally join a multicast group for reading, and use the same unicast credentials for writing; servers create either multicast or unicast outbound sockets according to the credentials provided, always listen on locally-defined ports for inbound traffic, and return kStreamNotConnected if no local credentials exist.  
Additional sub-clauses cover how to attach platform-specific socket-option strings and (D)TLS properties (restricted to 1:1 connections).  
Finally, it states that no default attribute values are defined and records the single semantic constraint that the AbstractRawDataStreamInterface.namespace attribute shall never exist.

nodes.title: Default Values
nodes.summary:
The excerpt specifies how AUTOSAR AP R25-11 RawDataStream servers and clients configure their communication sockets.  
Key points:  
- A server that has no bound local TCP port immediately returns kStreamNotConnected; if localTcpPort is mapped, the socket is created, bound and listened-to during RawDataStream construction and incoming connections are accepted via WaitForConnection().  
- Both client and server can list platform-specific socket options (e.g., SO_KEEPALIVE, SO_RCVBUF) in the socketOption attribute.  
- Secure channels are set with tlsSecureComProps, enabling (D)TLS only for 1:1 UDP/TCP connections; multicast or many-to-many use is excluded.  
- No default values are defined for any attribute.  
- A semantic constraint forbids the existence of AbstractRawDataStreamInterface.namespace.

nodes.title: Semantic Constraints
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how AUTOSAR AP R25-11 RawDataStream servers and clients are configured:

- A server that has no localTcpPort mapped cannot accept connections and will return kStreamNotConnected.  
- If localTcpPort is mapped, the constructor uses the EthernetCommunicationConnector credentials to create/bind/listen on a TCP socket; WaitForConnection() then accepts incoming requests.  
- Both client and server can list platform-specific socket options (e.g., SO_KEEPALIVE, SO_RCVBUF) in the socketOption attribute.  
- Secure channels are set with tlsSecureComProps, enabling (D)TLS for 1:1 UDP/TCP streams only.  
- No default values are defined.  
- A semantic constraint forbids the existence of AbstractRawDataStreamInterface.namespace.

nodes.title: Configurable Namespace for RawDataStream

It allows platform-specific socket options (e.g., SO_KEEPALIVE) via a string list attribute socketOption and (D)TLS properties via tlsSecureComProps, restricted to 1:1 connections. No default values are defined, and the namespace for AbstractRawDataStreamInterface must not exist.

nodes.title: IEEE1722TpConnection
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a formal specification (AUTOSAR AP R25-11) that defines three inter-related classes for configuring IEEE-1722 time-sensitive AVB/TSN streaming and IP-security in an Adaptive Platform network:

1. IEEE1722TpAcfConnection – concrete class for an “Audio/Control Format” stream:
   - Timing: acfMaxTransitTime gives the presentation-time offset added by the producer.  
   - Transport: acfTransportedBus aggregates one or more IEEE1722TpAcfBus instances (CAN, LIN, etc.).  
   - Triggering: collectionThreshold (byte count) and collectionTimeout (seconds) govern when an ACF message is sent.  
   - Mixing: mixedBusType boolean allows or forbids mixing different bus kinds in the same ACF packet.

2. IEEE1722TpAvConnection – abstract base for “Audio/Video” streams:
   - Adds maxTransitTime attribute (same semantic as above) and is specialized into AAF, CRF, IIDC, RVF sub-classes.

3. IEEE1722TpConnection – top-level abstract base for any 1722-TP stream:
   - Direction, destination MAC, VLAN priority, version.  
   - Stream-ID composed of macAddressStreamId + uniqueStreamId.  
   - Optional reference to a GlobalTimeDomain for time synchronization.

4. IPSecConfig & NetworkEndpoint – auxiliary security & addressing artefacts:
   - IPSecConfig holds global IPsec settings and a set of per-endpoint IPSecRule aggregates.  
   - NetworkEndpoint supplies the IP address or MAC multicast address, an optional FQDN, and may embed an IPSecConfig to secure the traffic.

All classes are UML-style meta-model elements (ARElement, ARObject, …) owned by an ARPackage and carry ATP status tags indicating they are still “candidate” specifications.

2. IPSecConfig class – groups global IPSec security properties (IPSecConfigProps) and a set of per-endpoint IPSecRule objects that provide end-to-end cryptographic protection for IP traffic.

3. NetworkEndpoint class – represents a network attachment point (IP or MAC multicast address) and optionally aggregates an IPSecConfig to secure its traffic; it also carries the fully-qualified domain name (FQDN) of the host.

The tables list each attribute’s type, multiplicity, kind, and usage notes, forming the machine-readable model that tooling will transform into runtime configuration for secure, time-synchronized AVB/TSN streams in adaptive ECUs.

1. IEEE1722TpConnection (abstract) – models a time-sensitive AVB/TSN stream, exposing attributes for direction, destination MAC, VLAN priority, stream-ID components (MAC + unique ID), version, and an optional reference to a global-time domain for synchronization.

2. IPSecConfig – groups IPsec security settings attached to a NetworkEndpoint; it contains one global IPSec property set and any number of per-endpoint IPSec rules/filters.

3. NetworkEndpoint – represents a network interface addressable by IP or MAC multicast; it carries the FQDN, frame priority, one or more network addresses (IPv4/IPv6/multicast), and an optional IPSecConfig for securing traffic.

4. PortInterface & PortPrototype (both abstract) – serve as base metaclasses for all component ports and interfaces in the Adaptive Platform, supporting namespace definitions, variability, and multiple port-specific annotations.

nodes.title: PortInterface
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a fragment of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 specification for the Raw-DataStream service.  
It defines three key meta-model elements:

1. NetworkEndpoint – models a network address (IPv4, IPv6 or MAC multicast) that can be attached to a Raw-DataStream endpoint; carries an optional frame-priority attribute (0-7).

2. PortInterface (abstract) – base class for every interface type a software-component port can provide or require; lists all existing interface subclasses and shows that a ServiceInterface can carry a hierarchical namespace definition (SymbolProps) used only on the Adaptive Platform.

3. PortPrototype (abstract) – base class for every port of an AUTOSAR software component; lists the various annotation kinds that can be attached to a port (client-server, delegated, IO-hw-abstraction, mode, NV-data, parameter).

nodes.title: PortPrototype
nodes.summary:
The excerpt is a formal UML-style specification fragment from the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform R25-11 “Raw Data Stream” document.  
It defines three meta-classes and their attributes/roles:

1. NetworkEndpoint – identifies a network address (IPv4, IPv6 or MAC multicast) and an optional frame-priority (0-7).  
2. PortInterface (abstract) – base class for all component-port interfaces; shows that a ServiceInterface can carry a hierarchical namespace via SymbolProps.  
3. PortPrototype (abstract) – base for provided/required component ports; lists all possible port annotations (client-server, sender-receiver, mode, trigger, NV-data, parameter, IO-hw-abstraction, delegated) and an optional PortPrototypeProps qualifier used only in the Adaptive Platform.  
4. Process – Adaptive-Platform-only descriptor for run-time executables, holding references to the Executable, optional design-time Process, restart policy, pre-load flag, functional-cluster affiliation, and state-machine/startup-config containers.

nodes.title: Process
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines two AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes:

1. PortPrototype (abstract) – qualifies port semantics via PropsPortPrototypeProps and carries collections of sender-receiver and trigger-port annotations; used only in the Adaptive Platform.

2. Process – groups runtime-execution metadata: design-time reference, one-or-more Executable references, functional-cluster affiliation, restart-attempt count, pre-load flag, standardized process-state machine, and state-dependent startup configurations; tagged as recommended package “Processes” and also Adaptive-only.

nodes.title: RPortPrototype
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines four AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes for raw-data streaming:

1. RPortPrototype – an “requires” port that references a PortInterface; owned by a SwComponentType.  
2. RawDataStreamClientInterface – client-side interface for un-serialized raw data streaming, implicitly offering connect, shutdown, write, read operations; packageable in ARPackage.  
3. RawDataStreamEthernetTcpUdpCredentials – configuration element (used in server unicast credentials) that optionally carries a TCP port number.  
4. RawDataStreamEthernetUdpCredentials – configuration element for UDP-only credentials, aggregated by several EthernetRawDataStreamRemoteClient/ServerConfig containers.

nodes.title: RawDataStreamClientInterface
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines three AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes for raw-data streaming:

- RPortPrototype – a component port that requires a RawDataStreamClientInterface; it references the interface it needs.  
- RawDataStreamClientInterface – the client-side port-interface that enables un-serialized raw-data streaming; it implicitly provides connect, shutdown, write and read operations.  
- RawDataStreamEthernetTcpUdpCredentials & RawDataStreamEthernetUdpCredentials – configuration objects that hold the network credentials (e.g., optional TCP port number) for establishing raw-data stream connections over TCP/UDP and UDP-only respectively.

nodes.title: RawDataStreamEthernetTcpUdpCredentials
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines three AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes for raw-data streaming:

- RPortPrototype – a component port that “requires” a PortInterface; it references the required interface via the attribute requiredInterface.  
- RawDataStreamClientInterface – the client-side PortInterface for un-serialized raw-data streaming; it implicitly offers connect, shutdown, write and read operations and is tagged for the RawDataStreamInterfaces package.  
- RawDataStreamEthernetTcpUdpCredentials & RawDataStreamEthernetUdpCredentials – configuration objects that hold network credentials for raw-data streams over TCP/UDP; the TCP variant adds an optional tcpPort attribute, while the UDP variant adds no new attributes.

nodes.title: RawDataStreamEthernetUdpCredentials
nodes.summary:
The excerpt defines three AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes for raw-data streaming:

1. RPortPrototype – a component “required” port that references a PortInterface; it is a blueprintable, identifiable prototype aggregated by SwComponentType.
2. RawDataStreamClientInterface – the client-side PortInterface for un-serialized raw-data streaming, implicitly offering connect, shutdown, write, and read operations; only used in the Adaptive Platform.
3. RawDataStreamEthernetTcpUdpCredentials & RawDataStreamEthernetUdpCredentials – configuration elements that hold network credentials for raw-data streams over TCP/UDP and UDP respectively; both are Adaptive-Platform-only descriptors aggregated inside Ethernet remote-server/client configurations (the TCP/UDP variant adds an optional tcpPort attribute).

nodes.title: RawDataStreamMapping

The excerpt defines three AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform meta-classes:

1. RawDataStreamMapping (abstract) – base for mapping raw, un-serialized data streams into an application; it can link a RawDataStreamDeployment, an input RPortPrototype, and the hosting Process.

2. RawDataStreamServerInterface – server-side interface that gives a software component the built-in abilities to waitForConnection, shutdown, write and read raw data streams without extra modeling.

3. TlsSecureComProps – TLS configuration element that references crypto-service primitives used for key exchange during the TLS handshake.

nodes.title: RawDataStreamServerInterface

2. RawDataStreamServerInterface – server-side interface providing built-in capabilities (waitForConnection, shutdown, write, read) for raw data streaming without additional modeling.

3. TlsSecureComProps – TLS security configuration containing key-exchange crypto primitives and a set of negotiable cipher suites for securing the stream.

The additions form three groups:

No constraints were changed or deleted in R25-11; the section only records the exhaustive list of new specification items added in R24-11.
